title: Self-Compressing Neural Networks
summary: This paper introduces Self-Compressing Neural Networks, where the network learns its own compression during training by making quantization bit-widths differentiable per-channel parameters. Each convolutional layer has learnable exponent (e) and bit-width (b) parameters alongside standard weights. A compression regularization term (λ·Q) is added to the task loss, creating a smooth tradeoff between model accuracy and model size. The straight-through estimator enables gradient flow through the non-differentiable rounding operation. During training, some channels have their bit-widths driven to near zero (effective pruning), while others maintain 2-4 bits. The reference implementation demonstrates this on MNIST, achieving ~98.2% test accuracy with ~18KB model size from an original 87,860 parameters.

key_concepts.name: Self-Compression via Learnable Bit-Widths
key_concepts.description: The core idea: each output channel in a convolutional layer has a learnable bit-width parameter (b) that determines how many bits are used to represent the weights in that channel. During training, the network jointly optimizes task performance and its own compression by making b a differentiable parameter optimized via gradient descent. Channels that are not useful get their bit-widths driven toward zero, effectively pruning them.
key_concepts.priority_rationale: This IS the paper. Without understanding learnable bit-widths, the entire self-compression mechanism is opaque. Must have exercises where students implement the b parameter, its ReLU activation, and observe channels being pruned.

key_concepts.name: Straight-Through Estimator (STE)
key_concepts.description: A technique for passing gradients through non-differentiable operations like rounding. In the forward pass, weights are quantized via rounding: qw.round(). In the backward pass, the gradient flows through as if rounding never happened. Implemented as: (qw.round() - qw).detach() + qw, which equals qw.round() in the forward pass but has gradient equal to the gradient of qw in the backward pass.
key_concepts.priority_rationale: The STE is the mathematical trick that makes the entire approach work. Without it, gradients cannot flow through the quantization operation, and the bit-width parameters cannot be learned. Students must implement this pattern and understand why .detach() is critical.

key_concepts.name: Quantized Weight Representation
key_concepts.description: Weights are quantized using two per-channel parameters: exponent e (scale) and bit-width b. The quantized weight is computed as: clip(2^(-e) * w, -2^(b-1), 2^(b-1)-1), then the dequantized weight used in convolution is 2^e * round(quantized_weight). The exponent e controls the scale/resolution of the quantization grid, while b controls the range (number of distinct values).
key_concepts.priority_rationale: Understanding the quantization formula is necessary to implement self-compression. Students need to work through the math of how e and b interact to define a quantization grid, and how this maps continuous weights to discrete values.

key_concepts.name: Compression-Accuracy Tradeoff via Regularization
key_concepts.description: The training loss combines task loss (cross-entropy) with a compression penalty: L = L_task + λ·Q, where Q is the average bits per weight across all layers. The hyperparameter λ (0.05 in the implementation) controls the tradeoff — higher λ produces smaller models with potentially lower accuracy. Q is computed as the sum of relu(b) * fan_in for each channel, divided by total weight count.
key_concepts.priority_rationale: The regularization term is what drives self-compression. Without it, bit-widths would never decrease. Students must implement Q computation and experiment with different λ values to see the Pareto frontier of size vs. accuracy.

key_concepts.name: Per-Channel Quantization Parameters
key_concepts.description: Unlike uniform quantization where all weights share the same precision, self-compression assigns independent (e, b) parameters to each output channel. This enables mixed-precision: important channels retain higher bit-widths while unimportant channels are quantized to near-zero bits. In the reference implementation, e is initialized to -8.0 and b to 2.0 per channel.
key_concepts.priority_rationale: Per-channel granularity is what makes self-compression adaptive. Students need to understand why per-channel is better than per-layer or per-network quantization, and observe the learned bit-width distribution across channels.

key_concepts.name: Emergent Channel Pruning
key_concepts.description: When the learned bit-width b for a channel approaches zero (via ReLU ensuring non-negative bits), that channel is effectively pruned from the network. This is an emergent property — the network decides which channels to prune without explicit pruning algorithms. The reference implementation shows some channels with b ≈ -0.009 (effectively 0 after ReLU).
key_concepts.priority_rationale: While not the central algorithm, emergent pruning is a key insight that connects self-compression to structured pruning literature. Students should analyze learned b values and identify pruned channels, but don't need separate exercises on pruning itself.

key_concepts.name: QConv2d Layer Implementation
key_concepts.description: A custom convolutional layer that wraps standard Conv2d with quantization. Stores weight, e (exponent), and b (bit-width) tensors. The forward pass: (1) compute quantized weights via clamping, (2) apply STE via round-detach trick, (3) apply convolution with dequantized weights. The qbits() method reports the effective number of bits used.
key_concepts.priority_rationale: This is the concrete implementation that students must build. The reference implementation provides the exact code pattern: weight initialization, qweight() computation, STE application, and bits counting. Students should reproduce this layer.

key_concepts.name: Convolutional Neural Network Architecture
key_concepts.description: The demonstration model is a CNN for MNIST with 5 QConv2d layers (1→32→32→64→64→10 channels), BatchNorm, max pooling, and ReLU activations. The final layer reshapes flattened features through a 1x1 QConv2d for classification. Total of 87,860 weight parameters.
key_concepts.priority_rationale: The architecture is the vehicle for demonstrating self-compression but is not novel. Students need to understand it to reproduce results, but the focus should be on the quantization mechanism, not CNN design.

key_concepts.name: Model Size Measurement
key_concepts.description: Model size is measured in bytes by converting the average bits-per-weight (Q) to bytes: model_bytes = Q/8 * weight_count. The reference achieves ~18,075 bytes from 87,860 parameters, representing roughly 1.64 bits per weight on average — a ~20x compression from 32-bit floats.
key_concepts.priority_rationale: Understanding how to measure compression is necessary for evaluating results, but the calculation itself is straightforward. Should appear in exercises as a metric computation step.

key_concepts.name: Training Dynamics Visualization
key_concepts.description: The reference implementation tracks test accuracy and model size (bytes) over 20,000 training steps. The dual-axis plot shows accuracy rising to ~98.2% while model size drops from ~22KB to ~18KB, illustrating the convergence behavior of joint optimization.
key_concepts.priority_rationale: Visualization helps students verify their implementation is working correctly and understand training dynamics. Should be a milestone in exercises but not a separate teaching concept.

key_concepts.name: Uniform vs. Non-Uniform Quantization
key_concepts.description: Self-compression produces non-uniform quantization across channels — the bit-width distribution is learned and varies. This contrasts with traditional approaches that apply uniform bit-widths across all layers or the entire network. The learned distribution reveals which parts of the network need more precision.
key_concepts.priority_rationale: While this distinction provides theoretical context, students can understand the core algorithm without comparing to uniform quantization in depth. Appropriate for a 'What's Next' discussion of quantization landscape.

key_concepts.name: Post-Training Quantization vs. Quantization-Aware Training
key_concepts.description: Self-compression is a form of quantization-aware training (QAT), where quantization is integrated into the training loop. This contrasts with post-training quantization (PTQ), which quantizes a pre-trained model. QAT generally achieves better accuracy at the same compression level because the model adapts to quantization errors during training.
key_concepts.priority_rationale: This comparison provides broader context for where self-compression fits in the model compression landscape, but is not needed to understand or implement the core algorithm. Belongs in introductory or concluding material.

prerequisites.name: Deep Learning Fundamentals
prerequisites.why_needed: Understanding neural network training (forward pass, backpropagation, loss functions, gradient descent) is essential since self-compression modifies the training loop with custom quantization and regularization.

prerequisites.name: Convolutional Neural Networks
prerequisites.why_needed: The paper and reference implementation use CNNs as the demonstration architecture. Students need to understand Conv2d operations, channels, kernel sizes, and feature maps to implement QConv2d.

prerequisites.name: PyTorch or Tinygrad Tensor Operations
prerequisites.why_needed: The implementation requires tensor operations including broadcasting, clamping, rounding, and detach(). Students must be comfortable with autograd mechanics, especially how .detach() affects gradient computation.

prerequisites.name: Binary Number Representation
prerequisites.why_needed: Understanding how bit-widths map to representable value ranges (e.g., b bits can represent 2^b distinct values) is needed to understand the quantization formula and why b controls precision.

prerequisites.name: Regularization in Machine Learning
prerequisites.why_needed: The compression penalty λ·Q is a regularization term. Students should understand L1/L2 regularization as analogies for how adding a penalty to the loss drives parameters toward certain values.

prerequisites.name: Basic Calculus (Chain Rule)
prerequisites.why_needed: Understanding why rounding blocks gradient flow and why the straight-through estimator works requires knowing the chain rule and the concept of a gradient being zero for piecewise-constant functions.

code_patterns.description: Straight-Through Estimator pattern: (qw.round() - qw).detach() + qw. In the forward pass this equals qw.round(), but in the backward pass gradients flow through qw directly, bypassing the non-differentiable rounding operation.

Per-channel quantization with learnable parameters: self.e and self.b are tensors shaped (out_channels, 1, 1, 1) that broadcast over weights. The quantized weight is: clip(2^(-e) * w, -2^(relu(b)-1), 2^(relu(b)-1) - 1).
Compression regularization in training loop: Q = sum(layer.qbits()) / weight_count computes average bits per weight, then loss = task_loss + lambda * Q penalizes high bit-widths during training.

Bits counting per layer: qbits() = relu(b).sum() * fan_in, where fan_in = product of weight dimensions except output channels. ReLU ensures non-negative bit-widths; channels with b ≤ 0 contribute zero bits.
concepts_demonstrated: ReLU as non-negativity constraint on learned parameters

code_patterns.description: Custom quantized convolution layer (QConv2d) with __init__ setting up weight, e, b parameters; qweight() computing quantized weights; and __call__ applying STE + convolution. The pattern of wrapping a standard operation with learnable quantization is reusable.

learning_goals: Understand how self-compressing neural networks learn their own quantization by making bit-widths differentiable per-channel parameters
Implement the straight-through estimator (STE) to enable gradient flow through rounding operations and explain why it works mathematically
Build a QConv2d layer with learnable exponent (e) and bit-width (b) parameters that quantizes weights during the forward pass
Design a training loop that jointly optimizes task performance and model compression via a regularization term λ·Q
Analyze learned bit-width distributions across channels to identify emergent pruning behavior and explain which channels are retained vs. pruned
Measure model compression ratios and reproduce the ~20x compression on MNIST while maintaining >98% test accuracy
Experiment with the compression coefficient λ to map the Pareto frontier of model size vs. accuracy
